senses.definition: to publicly support or recommend something

examples.x: The environmentalist passionately advocates for sustainable living practices.
The celebrity used their platform to advocate for social justice issues.
The organization advocates for mental health awareness, working to reduce stigma.
The doctor continues to advocate for healthier lifestyles to prevent chronic diseases.
Parents often advocate for improvements in the education system for the benefit of their children.

senses.definition: to estimate or assess the value, quality, or performance of something or someone

examples.x: She appraised his artwork and offered constructive feedback on how to improve his technique.
The manager appraised her team members' performance during the quarterly review.
They appraised the success of the project based on customer feedback.
The teacher appraised the student's performance during the presentation.
She appraised the benefits of the new plan before presenting it to the team.

senses.definition: to clearly and confidently say that something is the case

examples.x: In an interview last month, the athlete asserted that dedication and hard work will always lead to achieving fitness goals.
During the debate, the politician asserted their stance on the controversial issue.
In their groundbreaking research paper, the scientist had asserted the significance of their findings in advancing medical knowledge.

senses.definition: to unfairly influence or manipulate something or someone in favor of one particular opinion or point of view

examples.x: She biased the hiring process by favoring candidates from her alma mater.
It's important to avoid biasing the experiment by introducing preconceived notions.
The judge was accused of biasing the trial by showing favoritism towards the wealthy defendant.
The teacher tried not to bias her students' opinions by presenting a balanced view of the topic.
The advertising campaign was designed to bias consumers towards buying their product over competitors'.

senses.definition: to form an opinion by considering the information at hand

examples.x: He calculated that it would be more cost-effective to rent rather than buy.
She calculated that they could afford the new house with their current budget.
The team calculated that the weather would improve by the weekend.
After considering all options, he calculated that the best course of action was to wait.
They calculated that they would need additional staff to meet the deadline.

senses.definition: to state that one is bound to do something specific

examples.x: Upon joining the project, team members committed to meeting deadlines and delivering high-quality results.
The CEO publicly committed to donating a percentage of the company's profits to charitable causes each year.
In response to the crisis, the humanitarian organization committed to providing emergency relief and support to affected communities.
Before launching the new initiative, the team committed to conducting thorough research and gathering stakeholder feedback.

senses.definition: to exchange opinions and have discussions with others, often to come to an agreement or decision

examples.x: The board members will confer next week to finalize the budget for the upcoming fiscal year.
The teachers will confer during the staff meeting to develop a new curriculum for the next semester.
The negotiators needed to confer several times before reaching a consensus on the terms of the treaty.
The executives conferred late into the night to devise a strategy for the company's expansion.

senses.definition: to adjust oneself in order to align with new or different circumstances or expectations
to argue the truth of something

examples.x: The lawyer contended that her client was innocent based on new evidence presented in court.
He contended that climate change poses a serious threat to global ecosystems.
The historian contended that the ancient civilization was more advanced than previously believed.
Scientists contend that vaccination is the most effective way to prevent the spread of infectious diseases.
The politician contended that economic reforms would lead to greater prosperity for all citizens.

senses.definition: (of pieces of evidence, facts, statements, etc.) to be opposite or very different in a way that it is impossible for all to be true at the same time

examples.x: The witness's testimony contradicted the forensic evidence presented in court, leading to doubts about the accuracy of the case.
His account of the incident contradicts the version provided by other witnesses.
Her actions contradict her professed beliefs about environmental conservation.
The data from the study contradicted the initial hypothesis proposed by the researchers.
Can you please clarify why your statement contradicts the information provided in the report?

senses.definition: (of statements, beliefs, facts, etc.) incompatible or opposed to one another, even if not strictly illogical

examples.x: The two theories were contradictory, each offering a different explanation for the same phenomenon.
Her emotions seemed contradictory—laughing one minute and crying the next.
The contradictory evidence in the case made it difficult to reach a conclusion.
The plan had contradictory goals, aiming for both speed and precision.

senses.definition: to change the form, purpose, character, etc. of something

examples.x: She decided to convert the spare room into a home office for remote work.
The software allows users to convert files for compatibility.
They plan to convert the old warehouse into a modern art gallery.
The company will convert traditional paper records into a digital database for efficiency.

senses.definition: to determine by a process of logical reasoning

examples.x: From the evidence presented, the detective deduced the likely sequence of events.
Scientists deduced the presence of a new planet based on anomalies in the observed data.
Sherlock Holmes could deduce intricate details about a crime scene through careful observation.
By analyzing the symptoms, the doctor could deduce the likely cause of the illness.
Mathematicians use logical rules to deduce theorems from established axioms.

senses.definition: to consider in a particular manner

examples.x: The committee deemed the proposal worthy of further consideration.
The court deemed the evidence insufficient to support the case.
After the interview, they deemed her qualifications suitable for the position.
The teacher deemed the student's effort deserving of recognition.
The community deemed environmental preservation a top priority.

senses.definition: to argue with someone, particularly over the ownership of something, facts, etc.

examples.x: The two colleagues started to dispute the best approach to solving the project's challenges.
The parties involved disputed the terms of the agreement, leading to prolonged negotiations.
The neighbors began to dispute property boundaries, resulting in a heated argument.
The athletes disputed the referee's decision, claiming it was unfair and biased.

senses.definition: to repeat opinions or statements of another person, particularly to show support or agreement

examples.x: She echoed her colleague's concerns about the new policy, emphasizing the potential risks involved.
The students echoed the professor's statement about the importance of critical thinking in their essays.
Many community leaders echoed the mayor's call for unity and cooperation during the crisis.
The reporter's questions echoed the sentiments of the public, pressing the official for clear answers.
At the meeting, several board members echoed the CEO's vision for the future of the company, showing their support.

senses.definition: used to convey that one is against something
favorable or supportive in attitude or response

examples.x: In her role as a mentor, Sarah consistently provides affirmative guidance, empowering her mentees to pursue their goals with confidence.
The teacher's affirmative approach to student feedback fostered a classroom environment where students felt supported and confident in their abilities.
The committee's response to the proposal was affirmative, indicating their full support for the new initiative.
She received an affirmative nod from her supervisor, signaling approval to proceed with the project.
The senator's speech was met with affirmative cheers from the audience, showing widespread agreement with his views.

senses.definition: intending to provoke thought or discussion

examples.x: His challenging questions made everyone rethink their assumptions.
The film presented a challenging perspective on modern society.
She offered a challenging opinion that no one expected.

senses.definition: able to be believed or relied on

examples.x: After all the evidence was presented, her alibi seemed more credible than before.
Despite being a new researcher in the field, her extensive data and methodology made her findings credible.
The witness provided a credible account of the incident, which helped the jury reach a verdict.
To ensure the information was credible, the journalist verified her sources before publishing the article.
The expert's testimony was considered credible due to his extensive experience and qualifications in the field.

senses.definition: subject to argument or disagreement

examples.x: The decision to cut funding for the arts is highly debatable, with strong opinions on both sides.
Whether or not the law should be changed is a debatable issue among policymakers.
His actions in the situation are debatable, as some believe he made the right call while others disagree.
The fairness of the election process has been a debatable topic for years.

senses.definition: immediately noticed due to being apparent

examples.x: The patient's symptoms became more pronounced after the medication was discontinued.
The new policy had a pronounced impact on employee morale, leading to noticeable changes in productivity.

senses.definition: used to add a statement that contradicts what one has just said
used to convey that a statement can be supported with reasons or evidence

examples.x: He is arguably the best player in the league, consistently delivering outstanding performances.
The movie is arguably one of the most influential films of its genre, shaping future cinematic trends.
Arguably, the new policy has led to improved efficiency in the workplace, but not everyone agrees.
The novel is arguably a classic, with its profound impact on literature and culture.
Arguably, the recent changes to the city's infrastructure have contributed to a better quality of life for residents.

senses.definition: in a positive, approving, or useful manner

examples.x: The new policy was received favorably by employees, as it addressed concerns raised in previous feedback.
The candidate's qualifications and experience were reviewed favorably by the hiring committee.
The product was reviewed favorably by critics, praising its innovative features and high quality.
The economic indicators pointed favorably towards growth, with increasing employment rates and consumer spending.
Her presentation was received favorably by the audience, who appreciated her clear communication and engaging delivery.

senses.definition: used to introduce a statement that presents a truth or reality, often to clarify or emphasize something

examples.x: As a matter of fact, I did see him at the store earlier today.
He claimed it was the largest in the city, but as a matter of fact, there are several larger buildings in the downtown area.
You mentioned she wasn't interested; as a matter of fact, she expressed keen interest in joining the project
You may believe it's a rumor, but as a matter of fact, the company has officially announced the merger

senses.definition: used to introduce a second fact that must be taken into account

examples.x: She defended her thesis at the same time as managing a lab.
The committee approved the proposal at the same time as requesting revisions.

senses.definition: used to convey that what one is saying about something is also true for another related thing

examples.x: I don't like coffee, and, for that matter, I'm not a fan of tea either.
Anna refrained from using social media platforms, or any online forums, for that matter.
They didn't follow celebrity news or political updates, or any kind of current events, for that matter.

word_list.pronunciation: æz ɔːɹ sˈoʊ fˌɑːɹ æz ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪz kənsˈɜːnd

senses.definition: used to refer to the specific matter or topic being discussed or considered
to fight until a result is achieved or an agreement is reached

examples.x: The two nations decided to fight out their differences through diplomatic negotiations.
The siblings preferred to talk and find common ground rather than fight out their disagreements.
In the courtroom, lawyers will fight out the legal issues to seek justice for their clients.
Teams often have to fight out tough matches to determine the winner in competitive sports.
It's essential for couples to communicate openly and avoid fighting out every disagreement.

senses.definition: a serious argument between two sides caused by their different views and beliefs

examples.x: The clash between the two political parties escalated into a heated debate over healthcare reform.
The cultural festival was disrupted by a clash of opinions on how the event should be organized.
The courtroom was tense as the lawyers engaged in a clash over the interpretation of the evidence.
The clash between environmental activists and industrial developers resulted in a series of protests.
The board meeting ended abruptly due to a clash among the members about the future direction of the company.

senses.definition: a situation of hostility or strong disagreement between two opposing individuals, parties, or groups

examples.x: The heated confrontation between the neighbors stemmed from a dispute over property boundaries.
The diplomatic efforts aimed to prevent a military confrontation between the two nations.
The police were called to intervene in a confrontation between protesters and counter-protesters.
The teacher mediated a confrontation between two students who had been arguing during recess.
The heated confrontation in the courtroom arose from conflicting testimonies of the witnesses.

senses.definition: the quality of always acting or being the same way, or having the same opinions or standards

examples.x: Successful athletes attribute their achievements to discipline and consistency in training.
The chef's dishes are known for their exceptional flavor and consistency in presentation.
In leadership, consistency is key to building trust and maintaining morale among team members.
The company prides itself on the consistency of its product quality, ensuring customer satisfaction.
Her consistency in academic performance earned her recognition as the top student in the class.

senses.definition: a belief or opinion that is very strong

examples.x: Despite criticism, she held onto her conviction that renewable energy is the key to a sustainable future.
His conviction in the importance of education led him to establish a scholarship fund for underprivileged students.
The artist expressed his convictions through powerful paintings that portrayed themes of equality and justice.
Her deep religious convictions guided her moral decisions and actions throughout her life.
His conviction in the power of education inspired many students to pursue higher goals.

senses.definition: a detailed judgment of something, such as a work of art, a political idea, etc.

examples.x: The art professor provided a thorough critique of the student's painting, praising its composition while suggesting improvements to the color scheme.
Journalists offered a sharp critique of the government's economic policies, highlighting their potential impact on the middle class.
The literary critic published a thoughtful critique of the novel, analyzing its themes and character development.
The architect welcomed constructive critique from peers on the design proposal, aiming to refine the building's functionality.
Experts in environmental science conducted a comprehensive critique of the research findings, questioning the methodology and conclusions.

senses.definition: someone who does not provide a definite answer for a question, particularly when being asked in a poll

examples.x: During the market research, the interviewees were divided into three groups: those in favor, those against, and the don't-knows who remained undecided on the new technology.
In the survey, a sizable percentage of participants fell into the don't-know category when asked about their preferences for the new menu items.
The don't-knows in the focus group highlighted a need for additional information on the proposed changes to the workplace structure.
When asked about his favorite movie genre in the survey, John was a don't-know participant, offering no specific response.

senses.definition: a remark or opinion that has been used so much that it is not effective anymore
(of a comment) implying two distinct meanings

examples.x: Her double-edged remark about his promotion could be interpreted as both congratulatory and subtly critical.
The comedian's double-edged humor entertained some while offending others with its biting satire.
His double-edged compliment about her cooking skills left her uncertain whether he genuinely liked it or not.
The politician's double-edged statement on the economy was praised for its honesty but criticized for its potential to create confusion.
The article's double-edged critique of the company's policies highlighted both its achievements and areas needing improvement.

senses.definition: used when one is providing the general meaning of written or spoken statement instead of the exact words

examples.x: She made a suggestion to hire more staff, or words to that effect, to improve efficiency.
The CEO's memo indicated a need for budget cuts, or something to that effect, to stabilize finances.
He promised to finish the project by the end of the week, or a promise to that effect, ensuring timely completion.
The witness testified that he had seen the suspect leaving the scene of the crime, or testimony to that effect, providing crucial information to the jury.
The email instructed employees to prepare for the upcoming audit, or instructions to that effect, to ensure compliance with company policies.

senses.definition: used to show agreement, approval, etc.
used to describe something as really great, satisfying, or interesting

examples.x: After watching the movie, she exclaimed, "That film was awesomesauce!"
The concert last night was absolutely awesomesauce; the band played all their best songs.
The weekend getaway to the beach was totally awesomesauce; they had perfect weather and lots of fun activities.

senses.definition: used to show that one is angry, annoyed, or astonished

examples.x: For crying out loud, could you please turn down the volume on the TV?
Forget it, Rory, for crying out loud forget it.
It's my house, for crying out loud!
For crying out loud, can you please just answer the question?
I've asked you three times already, for crying out loud!

senses.definition: a story told in order to gain the sympathy of other people, particularly one that seems to be fake

examples.x: She tried to win the competition with a sob story about her hardships, but the judges saw through it.
The politician's sob story about his humble beginnings failed to sway voters who were more interested in his policies.
His constant sob stories about his difficulties at work began to sound repetitive and insincere.
The actress told a sob story to the media about her struggles with fame, hoping to gain public sympathy.
During the job interview, he avoided using a sob story and focused on his qualifications and experience instead.
